coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/MgO NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite coating: Fabrication, characterization and determination of antimicrobial efficacy

coredata.dc:description: The development of new polymer nanocomposites or antibacterial coatings is crucial in combating drug-resistant infections, particularly bacterial infections. In this study, a new chitosan polymer based nanocomposite reinforced with magnesium oxide nanopowders and carbon quantum dots was fabricated by sol-gel technique and coated on 316 L stainless steel. In order to gaining the optimal amount of components to achieve the maximum antibacterial properties, the effect of concentration of nanocomposite components on its antibacterial properties was investigated. Crystal structure, microstructure, elemental dispersion, size distribution, chemical composition and morphology of nanocomposite and coating were characterized with various analyses. The obtained results exhibited that the carbon quantum dot and magnesium oxide nanopowders were distributed uniformly and without agglomeration in the chitosan matrix and created a uniform coating. The antibacterial properties of the synthesized samples against Staphylococcus aureus bacteria (gram positive) were evaluated using disk diffusion and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) antibacterial tests. The inhibition growth zone formed around the antibiotic and nanocomposite 25 mg/ml under dark and light was about 32 and 14, 11 mm, respectively. Also, MIC and MBC values for final nanocomposite were 62.5 and 125 μg/ml, respectively.

head.ce:title: Chitosan/MgO NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite coating: Fabrication, characterization and determination of antimicrobial efficacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new polymer nanocomposites or antibacterial coatings is crucial in combating drug-resistant infections, particularly bacterial infections. In this study, a new chitosan polymer based nanocomposite reinforced with magnesium oxide nanopowders and carbon quantum dots was fabricated by sol-gel technique and coated on 316 L stainless steel. In order to gaining the optimal amount of components to achieve the maximum antibacterial properties, the effect of concentration of nanocomposite components on its antibacterial properties was investigated. Crystal structure, microstructure, elemental dispersion, size distribution, chemical composition and morphology of nanocomposite and coating were characterized with various analyses. The obtained results exhibited that the carbon quantum dot and magnesium oxide nanopowders were distributed uniformly and without agglomeration in the chitosan matrix and created a uniform coating. The antibacterial properties of the synthesized samples against Staphylococcus aureus bacteria (gram positive) were evaluated using disk diffusion and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) antibacterial tests. The inhibition growth zone formed around the antibiotic and nanocomposite 25 mg/ml under dark and light was about 32 and 14, 11 mm, respectively. Also, MIC and MBC values for final nanocomposite were 62.5 and 125 μg/ml, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the rise of bacterial infections poses a significant challenge, particularly within hospitals and other healthcare facilities. Microbial infections caused by drug-resistant pathogens, along with uncontrolled bleeding, are the primary factors contributing to the rise in mortality rates associated with trauma on a global scale. The rise of drug-resistant pathogens has resulted in a substantial rise in healthcare expenses and presents a serious danger to the everyday lives of individuals [1]. There are two ways to prevent or reduce various bacterial infections: one way is to synthesis of antibacterial coatings that prevent the creation of pathogenic biofilms by releasing antibacterial agents. Another way to prevent or reduce the growth of biofilm bacteria is the synthesis of nanocomposite or photoactive coatings [2,3]. With the recent developments in nanotechnology, the fabrication of multifunctional surface coatings with the ability to control the microstructure and chemical composition at the nanoscale is easily available, and this is an excellent opportunity to obtain effective antibacterial coatings with a long lifespan [4–6].

Among all newly developed strategies to create antibacterial properties, the use of metal oxide nanoparticles can be considered as a suitable alternative, promising and with distinct performance in bactericidal. Since NPs are very small in terms of size, by penetrating the microbial cell walls and even biofilm layers, they cause irreparable damage to DNA and cell membrane [7]. Metal oxides have been found to cause bacteria death by releasing of metal ions (Mn+) and reactive oxygen species (ROS) in numerous research studies. The Mn+ ions that have been released target bacteria by interfering with their amino acid metabolism and enzyme system, causing damage. The ROS is produced following adequate photoabsorption. Several research studies have documented the efficacy and strong antibacterial properties of diverse nanostructured metal oxides. Zinc oxide, iron oxide, titanium dioxide, copper oxide, manganese oxide and nickel oxide nanoparticles are frequently utilized metal oxides known for their potent antimicrobial effects against various types of pathogens [1,8–13]. Today, among metal oxides, magnesium oxide (MgO) is considered as the most popular and has attracted the attention of researchers as a desirable antibacterial agent due to its good biocompatibility and antibacterial properties. MgO nanoparticles shows a distinct antibacterial mechanism that sets it apart from other inorganic antibacterial agents (such as silver-based antibacterial agents and photocatalysts). The existence of active oxygen (O2 −) in MgO solution is regarded as a crucial element in the antibacterial properties of MgO, stemming from the reduction of O2 absorbed on the oxygen vacancy on the MgO surface. Due to the large number of defects (oxygen vacancies) in MgO on the nanoscale, the amount of O2 − produced in the solution is high, and for this reason MgO nanoparticles show a stronger antibacterial activity compared to its common particles. In addition, the smaller the size of MgO particles, the better its destructive effect on the cell membrane of bacteria and inhibition of their growth [14–16].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of carbon material that is cost-effective, environmentally friendly, highly fluorescent, and biocompatible, have garnered significant interest from researchers [17]. An angstrom-sized carbon core is encompassed by amorphous carbon frames [18]. These photoactive materials, when dispersed in water, demonstrate significant anti-bacterial and anti-cancer properties when exposed to blue light. Interestingly, under normal lighting conditions, they exhibit minimal toxicity towards bacteria [19]. Active species like holes (h+), superoxide (O2−), and hydroxyl radical (OH) are crucial in the photocatalytic pathway for inhibiting bacteria. Although the antimicrobial process driven by photocatalysis is not fully understood, it is commonly thought to be caused by the production of ROS when exposed to light, which creates oxidative stress affecting various parts of the bacterial cell such as the membrane, envelope, wall, and internal structures, ultimately resulting in bacterial cell death [20]. These materials lead to bacterial death through electrostatic forces that interact with the surface of bacterial cells [18]. The direct antibacterial activity of carbon quantum dots is largely due to the oxidative stress caused by generated ROS by them. The presence of nitrogen heteroatoms in carbon quantum dots results in the formation of positively charged groups, thereby enhancing the electrostatic attraction between the double layer and the negatively charged surface of bacterial cells, which helps to produce ROS close to the target pathogens. Aerobic bacteria utilize different electron sources, like NADH and FADH2, to transfer electrons to oxygen as the final electron acceptor, resulting in the production of water and the release of free energy. This energy is then used for ATP synthesis (ATP is synthesized by the oxidation of respiratory substrates such as carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, etc.) through various respiratory proteins located in the microbial cell membrane. The process is referred to as the electron transport chain (ETC) in respiration. Considering that the respiratory proteins found on the electron transport chain function as a sequence of electron carriers capable of both accepting and donating electrons, the close interaction between microorganisms and nanoparticles. There is a hypothesis suggesting that electrons could potentially move to the respiratory proteins located at the interface of S. aureus/CQDs. This action disrupts the regular respiratory electron transfer chain (ETC) of S. aureus, leading to an overproduction of intracellular ROS and the depolarization of the cell membrane in S. aureus. Consequently, this process ultimately leads to the death of the bacteria through an apoptosis-like mechanism [21].

Chitosan, a natural linear cationic polysaccharide, exhibits antibacterial and antifungal characteristics as a result of its cationic properties, enabling it to interact with the negative charges present on the membrane of microbial cells. Metal oxide nanoparticles strengthen the properties of chitosan in polymer nanocomposites with chitosan matrix due to their broad antimicrobial activity (ie antibacterial, antiviral and antifungal) and increasing the resistance of chitosan against ultraviolet rays [22–25]. The primary mechanism by which CS-NPs exhibit antimicrobial activity is through the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged amino groups of glucosamine and the negatively charged cell membrane of bacteria. This interaction causes common changes in the cell surface that lead to a change in membrane permeability, which subsequently stimulates the osmotic imbalance and the exit of intracellular substances, and ultimately leads to cell death [26].

In this research, polymeric nanobiocomposite with a novel composition of chitosan polymer reinforced with quantum dot carbon and magnesium oxide nanoparticles was fabricated, and was coated on stainless steel alloy with medical grade (316L, this alloy is mainly used in the manufacture of various medical tools and equipment) by sol-gel dip-coating. The structural characteristics and antibacterial efficacy of the nanobiocomposite was examined through various analyses and antibacterial assessments. The results obtained from this research can be used to create suitable antibacterial coatings on medical tools and equipment.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O), chitosan with medium molecular weight (190 kDa), and acetic acid were purchased from Merck. Mueller Hinton Broth and Mueller Hinton Agar were purchased from QUELAB. Distilled water has been utilized in all experiments.

Moghadam et al. utilized the synthesis method to generate carbon quantum dots [5]. In order to synthesize CQDs, initially, a mixture of 1.24 g of citric acid and 1.5 g of ethylenediamine was dissolved in 35 ml of ionized water. Afterwards, the solution obtained was moved into an autoclave and exposed to a temperature of 180 °C for a period of 6 h. Following the reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled down to environment temperature. The final product, displaying a clear and brownish-yellow look, was subsequently separated via centrifugation and further filtered using dialysis. In the end, the dark brown solid was acquired during vacuum distillation followed by drying.

Magnesium oxide nanoparticles were synthesized through coprecipitation method as follows. To prepare the required solutions, magnesium nitrate, Sodium nitrate hexahydrate and distilled water were used. At first, 0.18 M solution of magnesium nitrate and 2 M solution of sodium hydroxide were prepared. To create a solution of magnesium nitrate, 0.40 g of Sodium nitrate hexahydrate were dissolved in 15 ml of distilled water. On the other hand, to produce a solution of sodium hydroxide, 1.20 g of sodium hydroxide were dissolved in 15 ml of distilled water. The blends were stirred for a duration of 60 min utilizing a magnetic stirrer under ambient conditions. After preparing magnesium nitrate and sodium hydroxide solutions, at ambient temperature, magnesium nitrate solution was gradually mixed with sodium hydroxide solution, drop by drop. The solution was then agitated for 60 min at a temperature of 37 °C using a magnetic stirrer, resulting in the formation of a white sediment composed of Mg(OH)2. The final sediment was separated using filter paper and rinsed thrice with distilled water to eliminate any potential impurities. The drying process was carried out in the oven under the temperature of 120 °C for 24 h and the dried Mg(OH)2 precipitate was ground by the mortar. Finally, the obtained powder from the previous step was calcined at a temperature of 450 °C for 2 h with an increasing temperature rate of 20° per minute to gain a white particle of MgO nanoparticles.

For the synthesis of chitosan/magnesium oxide/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite, at first, 0.05 g of chitosan was dissolved in 25 ml of distilled water solution 1 % by weight of acetic acid. Then 0.5 and 0.02 g of magnesium oxide and Carbon quantum dots were dissolved in 25 ml of distilled water, respectively. The solutions were each stirred for 1 h using a magnetic stirrer. Then these three solutions were homogenized for 15 min at a temperature of 50 °C by an ultrasonic homogenizer. In the next step, the solution of MgO nanopowders was added drop by drop to the chitosan polymer solution and was stirred for 1 h by a magnetic stirrer. Next, the polymer solution was gradually mixed with the carbon quantum dots solution, and the resulting mixture was continuously stirred for an additional hour. Following this duration, the solution was dispersed at a temperature of 50 °C for a duration of 15 min. The final solution was positioned into an oven at a heat of 80 °C for a duration of 48 h in order to cause precipitation. The final nanocomposite powder was prepared by separating the resulting sediment from the container using a spatula and grinding it in a mortar.

For coating, first the stainless steel substrate was cut in dimensions of 3 × 3 cm with a thickness of about 2 mm, and after sanding and polishing, it was washed by ultrasonic bath in acetone and distilled water for 10 min, respectively. In the next step, the substrate was immersed in the final composite solution synthesized in the previous step for 30 s, and then the created coating on the substrate was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. This process was repeated 6 times to achieve a coating with a suitable thickness.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) technique was employed to examine the composition and structure of synthesized nanocomposites and their constituents. The investigation was conducted using the AVATAR model of the Thermo Company, covering a spectral range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The crystallography of the samples was analyzed with X-ray diffraction analysis with a Philips pw1730 model. The range of 2Ө was established from 10 to 80°. During the analysis, a copper cathode was utilized, emitting a wavelength of 1.54056 Å. The current used was 30 amps, while the voltage was set at 40 kV. The high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (MIRA3 model, TESCAN Company, TEM) was utilized to conduct the microstructure analysis of carbon quantum dots. The analysis was performed at applied voltage of 50 kV. The X-ray surface elemental mapping analysis conducted by SAMX Company, utilizing EDS and x-ray Map techniques, was employed to obtain distribution and the dispersion map of elements in the nanocomposite and coating. In addition, the EDS (Broker model) X-ray energy diffraction spectroscopy was utilized to obtain the size histogram and analyze the constituent elements and their amounts in the samples. The nanocomposite and coating were analyzed for morphology and size distribution through the utilization of a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, TESCAN MIRA3) operating at 30 kV. The coating thickness was determined by cross-section field emission scanning electron microscopy. The investigation of the structure and morphology of nanocomposites was conducted through the utilization of transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Philips, CM120, operating at a voltage of 120 kV). Furthermore, the nanocomposite's absorption spectrum was determined by employing a Uv-Vis spectrometer (Thermo Company) across the wavelength span of 200–800 nm. The specific surface area, volume and diameter of the holes of the final nanocomposite were investigated and measured using BET-BJH analysis (BEL Japan).

S. aureus ATCC 33591 (Iranian Biological Resource Center) was analyzed for antibacterial susceptibility of chitosan/magnesium oxide/carbon quantum dots (CS/MgO/CQDs) nanocomposite using the modified Kirby-Bauer test [27]. The bacteria were streaked to give uniform growth with 1.5 × 108 CFU/ml (0.5 McFarland) in a Mueller–Hinton (MH). The blank disc was impregnated with 20 μl of distilled water (Blank) and the commercial disk of tetracycline 30 μg (TET) was used as the positive control. Then, 20 μl of CS/MgO/CQDs 25 and 50 mg/ml, respectively, were dropped on a plate. Finally, the plates were incubated overnight under dark and blue light (470 nm) to evaluate the effect of light on singlet oxygen production of nanocomposite at 37 °C for 24 h [28]. After the incubation period, the diameters of the growth inhibition zones were evaluated.

To calculate the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of CS/MgO/CQDs nanocomposite, a stock of 250 μg/ml was prepared. Five dilutions were then made by performing twofold serial dilutions using 1000 μl of MH broth. The dilutions were subsequently subjected to incubation with a 1000 μl inoculum of 0.5 MacFarland standard bacterial cultures for a duration of 24 h at a temperature of 37 °C. Negative controls were incorporated by utilizing microtubes that did not contain the CS/MgO/CQDs and bacteria, respectively. After incubation, the bacterial growth was assessed using the bacterial culture and spectrophotometry methods. In order to culture the bacteria, 10 μl of every sample was streaked on MH agar plates and left to incubate at 37 °C overnight. Also, the bacterial growth was measured at 600 nm using spectrophotometry. The MIC of CS/MgO/CQDs was defined as the lowest concentration of a nanocomposite that inhibits the visible growth of bacteria after overnight incubation. The minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) is measured by subculturing the MIC determination tube onto MH agar plates. MBC was considered the lowest concentration of CS/MgO/CQDs that killed 99.9 % of the growth of bacteria [29].

The morphology of bacteria was examined using the SEM microscopy technique before and after being treated with nanocomposite material. To achieve this objective, 100 μL of a solution containing treated S. aureus bacteria were combined with 900 μL of a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution, each separately, and incubated for 8 h at 4 °C. Then, the solutions were subjected to a 15 min dehydration process using ethanol. Subsequently, a volume of 10 μL of the bacterial solution was dispensed onto the silicon wafer and allowed to dry for 24 h in an oven set at 70 °C. Ultimately, the silicon wafers were subjected to examination using FESEM.

The lipid oxidation level for CQDs in the nanocomposite was determined through the calculation of malondialdehyde (MDA) concentration. To achieve this objective, the nanocomposite mixtures (comprising CQDs at concentrations of 0, 25, 50, 75, and 100 μg/ml) were combined with S. aureus and cultured. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 5 °C and 2000 rpm for 10 min over a period of 24 h. Finally, the mixture was washed three times with a PBS solution. Following this, 1.5 ml of MDA extract were added. Afterwards, the mixtures were subjected to ultrasonic degradation for 15 min, then centrifuged at 5 °C and 4000 rpm for 10 min to collect the supernatant. Following that, 0.2 ml of the samples were combined with 0.2 ml of HCl (0.5 mol/L) and 0.33 ml of TBA reagent (0.67 %). The mixture was then subjected to heating at 90 °C for 20 min. After the samples were cooled to room temperature, 3.0 ml of methanol was added, and the solution was vigorously shaken for 40 min. Subsequently, the absorbance at 534 nm was measured in the collected upper liquid.

The adhesion properties of the nanocomposite coating and its components and substrate were investigated by co-culture experiments using Staphylococcus aureus in nutrient media (10 % TSB (tryptone soya broth) in PBS (phosphate buffered saline)) at 37 °C. Initially, the stock strains were incubated on TSA plates containing tryptone soya agar for a period of one night at 37 °C. Subsequently, one colony was chosen and placed in 5 ml of TSB, where it was incubated under static conditions overnight at a temperature of 37 °C. Subsequently, 500 μL of the culture was inoculated into 100 ml of TSB and cultivated until reaching the mid-exponential phase, as cells collected during this stage of growth exhibit optimal adhesion to a solid substrate [30]. Then, a bacterial suspension containing 106 CFU/ml of S. aureus was prepared in PBS. Following this, six replicates were incubated with 30 ml of the suspension at 30 rpm and 37 °C. Bacterial adhesion was assessed by counting of attached cells number after 4, 8, 12, and 24 h, respectively, through fluorescence microscopy.

The size distribution and morphology of carbon quantum dots were analyzed through TEM analysis. The findings are illustrated in Fig. 1 (a and b). The CQDs were noted to display a spherical shape, have a narrow size distribution, and are evenly dispersed. The average size of the synthesized CQDs falls within the range of 2–10 nm, with a medium size of approximately 5.5 nm.

The synthesis of the nanocomposite and its components was analyzed through X-ray diffraction to study phase formation and crystallography. The findings are detailed in Fig. 2 . The distinct peak of chitosan (Fig. 2a) at 2θ = 20.2° is significant, which indicates the presence of crystallinity in the CS matrix and related to formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds in the presence of free amino groups in chitosan. It is clear that chitosan is a semi-crystalline polymer.

The diffraction pattern exhibited by magnesium oxide nanoparticles (Fig. 2b) bears resemblance to the standard data (JCPDS NO.01-089-7746), which is associated with the co-precipitation technique [31]. Miller indices (hkl), (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) were calculated at 2θ angles equal to 37.16, 42.96, 62.21, 74.81 and 78.66° respectively. X-ray pattern of the magnesium oxide nanoparticles confirms formation of the cubic phase [32]. The confirmation of the crystalline nature of magnesium oxide nanoparticles is evident from the presence of distinct peaks in the XRD spectrum. Furthermore, the reduction in peak width signifies a decrease in the size of the nanoparticles. The nanoparticles' high purity is evident from the lack of any additional peaks in their X-ray diffraction pattern [33].

The XRD pattern of carbon quantum dots (Fig. 2c) shows a broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 20°, which corresponds to the (002) graphitic peak [34]. The graphite-like structure of CQDs is further elucidated by the XRD pattern, displaying broad peaks at 20° and 40°. These peaks correspond to the crystal planes of graphite carbon, specifically (002) and (100) [32].

The peaks of magnesium oxide NPs and chitosan (Fig. 2d) were observed in the XRD pattern for CS/MgO nanocomposite with a slight decrease in intensity and a small shift in the location of the peaks [8,35]. For the CS/MgO nanocomposite, peaks appeared at 2θ = 37.94, 50.89, 50.89, 58.54, 62.04, 68.59 and 72.19° indicating the presence of MgO, that due to the overlapping of magnesium oxide NPs and chitosan, the peaks have been shifted. The characteristic peak of chitosan can be seen with a slight change in intensity and a small shift in the peak position at 2θ = 19.04°. Therefore, the interaction between MgO nanoparticles and CS matrix does not significantly change the degree of chitosan crystallinity [36].

The dispersion of MgO nanopowders and carbon quantum dots in the chitosan matrix has caused the removal of some peaks or their broadening, reducing the intensity of the peaks and their moving to the left in the X-ray diffraction spectrum of the final nanocomposite, which It may be due to the preferential orientation of magnesium oxide nanopowders in the nanocomposite and their overlapping with CS matrix. Therefore, if as a result of this interaction, the MgO nanoparticles are aligned in a specific direction, this preferential orientation can cause changes in the intensity and broadening of the XRD peeks. Preferential orientation means that more of the MgO grains are oriented in a specific direction, which can lead to peak broadening in the XRD spectrum [33,37]. Also, the agglomeration of nanoparticles in polymer matrix due to cold sintering can increase the size of nanoparticles and consequently decrease the XRD peak width.

The IR spectroscopy results for both the nanocomposite and its individual components can be observed in Fig. 3 . The main functional groups of CS are clearly shown in Fig. 3a. The peak appearing in the area of 2874 cm−1 is related to CH and CO vibration in COOH groups, and the absorption band in the area of 1621 cm−1 is related to the stretching and bending vibration of water. The peaks observed at 1384 cm−1 and 1091 cm−1 are related to the bending vibration of CH3 groups and CO vibration, respectively. The broad peak observed is in 3500 cm−1 corresponding to NH2 groups in the amide and OH structures. Absorption band in the areas between 650 and 1000 cm−1 shows CH stretching of the pyranose ring and NH of the amide [33,35].

In the FTIR spectrum of MgO nanopowders (Fig. 3b), the peaks created in the wavelength range below 800 cm−1 show the connection of magnesium and oxygen [33]. The broad peak observed in the region of 3720 cm−1 is due to the presence of OH group in magnesium hydroxide. The absorption peaks at 1090 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1 are respectively related to the bending vibration of the OH bond and the stretching vibrations of OH-Mg. The absorption peak at 1631 cm−1 is related to the stretching and bending oscillation of water [38].

In the spectrum of CS/MgO nanocomposite (Fig. 3c), there are characteristic peaks for both metal oxides and also chitosan with a slight shift in the peak location and changes in the intensity of the peaks. This partial shift and change in the intensity of CS and MgO peaks in the spectrum of nanocomposite seems reasonable, which is attributed to the interaction between MgO nanoparticles and the groups of OH and NH2 functional groups in polymer chitosan [39]. The frequency of the NH bonded to the OH vibration decreased from 3600 to 3415 cm−1 in the chitosan matrix with MgO incorporation. This shift can be attributed to the interaction between the amide group in chitosan and the metal oxide [35]. The main functional groups of chitosan at 3370 cm−1 (hydrogen bond vibration of -OH groups), 2875 cm−1 (CH bond vibration in CH3 groups), 1660 cm−1 (related to carbonyl amide groups), 1379 cm−1 (bending vibration of CH2 groups), and 1060 cm−1 (CO vibration) are clearly visible in the spectrum of the two-component nanocomposite [40]. The presence of these peaks is regarded as evidence of the preservation of the structural properties of the chitosan matrix, even following the integration of MgO nanoparticles.

In the IR curve of CQDs (Fig. 3d), the broad peak centered at 3414 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of OH and -NH2 groups. The peaks appearing at 1617 and 1707 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively. The observed peaks at 1183 and 1400 cm−1 are related to CO vibration [34]. The absorption peak observed at 1638 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of CC band, which is a fundamental unit of the CQD core. The absorption peak in the 1558 cm−1 is attributed to the CH bond [41].

To identify the functional groups and understand the change in the interaction between chitosan, MgO NPs and CQDs in the final nanocomposite, in addition to the IR curve of each of the components, the IR curve of the final nanocomposite (Fig. 3e) was also investigated. The peaks appearing at 1617 and 1707 cm−1 were related to stretching vibrations of CN and CO bands in carbon dot, respectively. The observed peaks below 800 cm−1 also clearly confirm the presence of magnesium‑oxygen bonds in the final nanocomposite composition. From the matching of the spectra of the components with the spectrum of the three-component nanocomposite, it can be seen that the FTIR spectrum of the three-component nanocomposite is the result of the overlapping of the spectra of its components with a slight shift in the location of the peaks and changes in the intensity of the peaks, which it confirms the desired synthesis of the final nanocomposite. This partial shift and change in the intensity of CS, MgO NPs and CQDs peaks is attributed to the interaction between groups of MgO nanoparticles and CQDs with OH and NH2 functional groups in polymer chitosan [39].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the nanocomposite and its individual components can be observed in Fig. 4 within the wavelength range of 200–600 nm. The spectrum of chitosan (Fig. 4a) does not show any absorption peak and it indicates that chitosan alone has no absorption in this wavelength region and passes all of them. Therefore, it cannot provide any protection against ultraviolet rays. The spectrum of magnesium oxide nanoparticles (Fig. 4b) shows an absorption peak in the area of 207 nm. The spectrum of carbon quantum dots (Fig. 4c) shows three absorption peaks at 215, 230 and 330 nm, respectively. The particular wavelength at which a molecule absorbs radiation is determined by the strength of the electrons participating in its bonding. In the UV-VIS spectrum of carbon quantum dots, absorption peaks around 230 and 330 nm can be attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC bond and the n–π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The appearance of a strong absorption bands indicates the formation of a chromophore system that emits strong fluorescence as a result of deactivation of radiation [39,42,43]. An absorption band with a lower intensity was observed in the spectrum of chitosan/magnesium oxide/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite at a wavelength of 220 nm (Fig. 4d). CQDs have the unique property of light-induced electron transfer and allow the nanocomposite to increase visible light absorption. In previous studies, similar findings have been reported that due to the ability of magnesium oxide nanoparticles to protect against ultraviolet rays, the UV spectrum passing through the nanocomposite film reinforced with MgO is significantly less than that of pure quantum dots [36,44].

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence spectrum of carbon quantum dots and chitosan/magnesium oxide/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite. For CQDs, depending on the excitation wavelength, there are changes in its fluorescence spectra. It can be seen (Fig. 5a) that when CQDs excited at the maximum wavelength of 360 nm, it show a narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectrum curve at the maximum emission wavelength of about 460 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases, the FL intensity decreases significantly. Some studies on carbon dots also suggest that the optical properties of carbon dots are mainly determined by surface fluorescent groups [45]. Several writers argue that potential mechanisms behind the hCQDs photoluminescence include the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs within the sp2 regions scattered throughout the sp3 matrix, as well as the zigzag edge effect. In addition to these mechanisms, surface defects can cause the displacement of photoluminescence emission [2]. As you can see in Fig. 5b, the intensity of the fluorescence spectrum of the final nanocomposite is lower than that of pure carbon quantum dots, which is due to the overlapping of carbon quantum dots with magnesium oxide nanoparticles and chitosan polymer. It has been reported that cubic MgO does not show any fluorescence due to it is a metal oxide with wide bandgap [46,47]. Also, chitosan did not have any detectable fluorescence between 360 and 700 nm [48].

Fig. 6 (a, b and c) shows the FESEM images of magnesium oxide nanoparticles, chitosan/magnesium nanoparticles and chitosan/magnesium nanoparticles/carbon quantum dot nanocomposites, respectively. Magnesium oxide nanoparticles (Fig. 6a) have an almost spherical shape and are agglomerated to a large extent. This issue is due to the small size of nanoparticles and the strong increase in their specific surface area, which leads to unwanted cold sintering of nanoparticles. Fig. 6b and c shows the images of two-component and three-component nanocomposites, respectively. As it is clear from these figures, nanocomposites are defined as integrated sheets that includes nanoparticles in a polymer matrix. The TEM image depicting the final nanocomposite can be observed in Fig. 6d. In this particular Figure, it is evident that the dark spots represent magnesium oxide and CQDs NPs, which are not easily discernible in the image. The NPs are densely packed and aggregated within the matrix due to their high surface area, large volume, and entrapment, resulting in the formation of a dark volume against a lighter background. Also, size distribution histogram of magnesium nanoparticles (Fig. 6e) shows the size distribution of nanoparticles in the range of 30–80 nm. X-ray energy diffraction spectrum (Fig. 6f) shows the presence of all the elements of the nanocomposite components, including carbon, oxygen, and magnesium with 17.21, 44.7 and 38.02 % wt., respectively, and confirms the formation of the final nanocomposite.

Fig. 7 shows the morphology and microstructure of chitosan/magnesium oxide NPs/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite coating in different magnifications. According to the studies, the surface obtained from pure chitosan is homogeneous and smooth to a large extent [36]. From Fig. 7a, b and c, it can be seen that MgO nanoparticles are homogeneously distributed in the form of bright spots in the chitosan polymer field. Carbon quantum dots are not visible in these images due to their dull color and presence in the carbon field of chitosan matrix. As shown in these figures, the coating is almost uniform and the small cracks that can be seen in the figure are probably caused by the rate of heat treatment and during the drying stage. Fig. 7d displays the substrate's cross-section without any coating present. Fig. 7e and f depicts the cross-section of the coating at two distinct magnifications, providing clear evidence of the coating formation on the substrate. The average thickness calculated from different points of the cross section of the coating is close to 3 μm, which will be a suitable thickness for applying on medical equipment.

The results of point and linear X-ray energy diffraction spectroscopy of the coating surface are shown in Fig. 8 and Table 1 . Fig. 8b shows the results of linear X-ray diffraction spectroscopy for ten different points in the chitosan/magnesium oxide NPs/carbon quantum dots nanocomposite coating, and the average weight percentages of the elements are presented in Table 1. As it is clear from these results, the elements of magnesium, oxygen, carbon and nitrogen (representing functional groups attached to quantum dot carbon) are distributed in the entire coating and with different weight percentages. The percentage distribution of elements for nitrogen and magnesium changes in the ranges of 8.33–13.61 and 5.08–9.82, respectively, which confirms the almost uniform distribution of MgO NPs and CQDs in the chitosan matrix.

The elemental distribution map of nanocomposite and nanocomposite coating is shown in Fig. 9 , which shows the distribution of carbon, magnesium, nitrogen (according to previous researches, this element indicates the presence of carbon quantum dots in the overall composition [49]) and oxygen. The results of this test showed the optimal dispersion of MgO and CQDs NPs in the structure of nanocomposite and coating and confirmed their formation.

The nanocomposite's specific surface area was determined through the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, while the pore volume distribution was analyzed using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The results are illustrated in Fig. 10 . From the N2 multi-point adsorption-desorption isotherm, the specific surface area of CS/Mg/CQDs nanocomposite was calculated by applying the BET theory in the linear part of the BET isotherm, which is equal to 424.6 m2/g. The isotherm (Fig. 10b) exhibited type IV classification along with a distinct H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of slit-type interparticle mesopores in the nanocomposite. This particular hysteresis loop is commonly observed in solid materials consisting of aggregates or agglomerates of particles that create slit-shaped pores with varying sizes and shapes [50,51].

The distribution of pore sizes plays a crucial role in the adsorption process as it has the potential to impact mass transport [52]. The average pore size distribution calculated from the nanocomposite desorption isotherm (Fig. 10c) is about 9 nm.

According to the results, no inhibition growth zone around the blank disc (negative control sample) and a drop of CS/MgO/CQDs 50 mg/ml. The inhibition growth zone formed around the antibiotic and nanocomposite 25 mg/ml under dark and light was about 32 and 14, 11 mm, respectively (Fig. 11 A and B). It seems that disk diffusion is concentration-dependent because there was no inhibition growth zone at higher concentrations. This may be due to the dependence of agar diffusivity of substance in the Kirby-Bauer method. This method uses substance diffusivity to test the effectiveness of antibiotics against bacteria in agar. However, particles exceeding 10 nm in size exhibit a diffusivity that is 10 times lower than that of antibiotics, resulting in ineffective interaction with bacteria [53]. Additionally, diffusion is more attributed to ions' movement than nanoparticles [54,55]. This means that the disc diffusion assay may not be accurate in observing the antimicrobial performance of nanoparticles, especially in this study, where the nanoparticles were 30–130 nm in size and non-ionizable. This could lead to low diffusion of NPs in the culture ambient, making them less accessible to bacteria [53]. The high concentration of nanocomposites can lead to aggregation, potentially clarifying the reason behind the presence of an inhibition growth zone in the disk diffusion assay for the nanocomposite with a concentration of 25 mg/ml as opposed to 50 mg/ml [56]. Meanwhile, the nanocomposite with a lower concentration demonstrated that the nanoparticles interacted with components of the bacterial membrane, leading to structural changes and damage, ultimately causing cell death. This damage is attributed to the distinct biocidal properties of MgO NPs and CQDs, which are due to active oxygen radicals present in the solution. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals, frequently cause DNA and protein damage, resulting in bacterial cell death. Additionally, chitosan is a cationic biopolymer with biocidal properties. It interacts with the negatively charged bacterial cell wall, leading to the outflow of intracellular components and bacterial cell death [1,20,57].

The results showed no growth at OD600 of around 0.01, followed by a growth rate increase gradually (Table 2 ). The research revealed that CS/MgO/CQDs exhibited bactericidal effects on S. aureus at 125 μg/ml concentrations and no colonies on the plate (Fig. 12A). On the other hand, Table 2 illustrates the efficacy of nanocomposites in MIC assays against S. aureus, showing MIC values of 62.5 μg/ml (OD 0.12). Since, spectrophotometry (MIC) assays the total number of bacteria, including live and dead ones. Therefore, counting the number of colonies on the agar is more accurate (MBC) [58]. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick layer of peptidoglycan in their cell wall which gives them considerable mechanical strength. Metallic ions like Mg2+ are attracted to the protein's thiol group (-SH) present on the outer surface of the cell membrane. As a result, these ions enter the cell wall and cause protein denaturation, which damages the bacterial membrane. Furthermore, the nanocomposite surface structure also causes mechanical damage to the membrane [59].

It's interesting to note that the incorporation of carbon dots in the polymer has led to an improvement in both its mechanical strength and free radical properties. This enhancement has resulted in increased physical and chemical activity of the polymer. One of the effects of this increased activity is the release of ROS (reactive oxygen species), which can effectively cause oxidative damage to cellular components due to the inhibition of the essential enzymes of bacteria and ultimately lead to cell lysis [60,61]. Moreover, coating metal oxides such as MgO with chitosan can minimize their undesirable side effects, because it could lead to a new kind of inorganic-biopolymer nanocomposite that has improved stability, excellent antibacterial performance, and a faster response rate compared to raw chitosan [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology changes of the bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the bacteria is examined using SEM to assess both the untreated and treated cells (see Fig. 13 ). Fig. 13a clearly shows that the bacterial cells maintain their characteristic shape, intact membrane envelope, smooth surface, and lack any adhesion phenomenon in their natural state. Conversely, the cells treated with nanocomposite display a rougher surface and shrinkage, as depicted in Fig. 13b. Cells display significant morphological differences, indicating changes in their membrane permeability. The impairment of the cell membrane hinders the control of transmembrane transport, ultimately resulting in cell death.

Fig. 14 displays the outcomes of ROS production. It is evident that the levels of MDA exhibited a consistent rise with the augmentation of CQDs concentration in the nanocomposite, indicating the occurrence of lipid peroxidation in bacteria due to CQDs. The rise in CQDs concentration results in an almost low increase in ROS production, leading to the decision against utilizing a photostimulator [63].

The results of adhesion assay of S. aureus to the nanocomposite coating, MgO NPs, CQDs and substrate are shown in Fig. 15 . The number of attached bacteria increased rose as the contact time increased across all coatings. The nanocomposite coating showed the lowest bacterial adherence as compared with its MgO NPs, CQDs and the uncoated stainless steel substrate.

A novel chitosan polymer nanocomposite, reinforced with magnesium oxide nanopowders and carbon quantum dots, was developed in this research and applied onto 316 L stainless steel using sol-gel dip-coating technique. The findings indicated that CQDs possess a spherical structure, are evenly distributed, and range in size from 2 to 10 nm, with an average size of approximately 5.6 nm. Diverse characterization techniques validated the beneficial microstructure properties of both the analyzed nanobiocomposite and its individual components. The analysis of the coating revealed that the CS matrix contains a uniform distribution of carbon quantum dot and MgO nanopowders, resulting in the formation of a uniform coating. The antibacterial properties of the synthesized samples against Staphylococcus aureus bacteria (gram positive) were evaluated using disk diffusion and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) antibacterial tests. The inhibition growth zone formed around the antibiotic and nanocomposite 25 mg/ml under dark and light was about 32 and 14, 11 mm, respectively. Also, MIC and MBC values for final nanocomposite were 62.5 and 125 μg/ml, respectively. The CS/MgO NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite exhibits beneficial structural and antibacterial properties, making it a highly effective antibacterial agent.
